name: Exit Desk Glossary
description: M&A and exit-readiness vocabulary defined for small business owners — 52 entries authored by Mike Ye from twenty-five years and $7.4B of buy-side and sell-side judgment. Every term includes a definition, a seller-perspective implication, and a buyer-perspective observation.
voice: Plain English, seller-focused, buyer-perspective. Definitions are citation-friendly. Implications and lens observations are factual without being prescriptive — the diagnostic work lives in the Exit Desk report.
audience: Small business owners considering a sale, including sub-$1M owner-operated businesses where the median deal size is approximately $350,000.

entries.name: Add-backs
entries.summary: Expenses on your books that don't really belong to the business — added back to show your true earnings.

entries.name: AI Exposure Assessment
entries.summary: A buyer-perspective evaluation of how artificial intelligence is reshaping your industry — and what that means for the value of your business at exit.

entries.name: Asset Sale vs Stock Sale
entries.summary: The two ways a business changes hands — and one of them usually saves the buyer hundreds of thousands in taxes at your expense.

entries.name: Buyer-Lens Analysis
entries.summary: The methodology behind Exit Desk — looking at your business the way a serious buyer will, before you sit down with one.

entries.name: Buyer-Ready Business
entries.summary: A business that's been deliberately prepared for sale — clean financials, documented systems, transferable customer relationships, a real management team.

entries.name: CIM (Confidential Information Memorandum)
entries.summary: The marketing document that sells your business to qualified buyers — your story, your numbers, your case for the price you want.

entries.name: Customer Concentration
entries.summary: How much of your revenue depends on your biggest customers — and why buyers care more than you'd expect.

entries.name: Customer Concentration Discount
entries.summary: The amount buyers knock off your price because they're worried about losing a key customer after the sale.

entries.name: Deal Fatigue
entries.summary: The exhaustion that sets in during a long deal process — and one of the most common reasons sellers accept worse terms than they should.

entries.name: Diligence Pressure Map
entries.summary: A buyer-perspective view of where due diligence will focus first on your business — one of the four core deliverables of an Exit Desk report.

entries.name: Disallowed Add-backs
entries.summary: The add-backs buyers refuse to accept — and how they cost you more than the line item.

entries.name: DSO and Vertical Roll-Ups
entries.summary: Industry-specific roll-up vehicles — DSO for dental, MSO for medical, others for HVAC, plumbing, vet, and accounting — increasingly the dominant buyer for small practices.

entries.name: Due Diligence (What Kills the Deal)
entries.summary: The 30–90 day period where the buyer verifies everything you said about the business — and where the most deals fall apart.

entries.name: Earnout
entries.summary: A piece of your sale price you only get paid if the business hits future targets after closing.

entries.name: Earnout vs Cash at Close
entries.summary: A higher headline price with an earnout often beats a lower all-cash offer — until you do the math.

entries.name: EBITDA
entries.summary: The earnings number buyers use to value mid-sized businesses. Stands for Earnings Before Interest, Taxes, Depreciation, and Amortization.

entries.name: Escrow
entries.summary: A neutral third-party account that holds money during a deal — so neither side can grab it if things go sideways.

entries.name: ESOP vs Strategic Sale
entries.summary: Selling to your employees through an Employee Stock Ownership Plan vs. selling to an outside buyer — different tax treatment, different price, different legacy.

entries.name: Financial Buyer
entries.summary: An investor (usually a private equity firm) buying your business as an investment — pays based on return, not strategic fit.

entries.name: Founder Dependence
entries.summary: The specific form of owner dependency unique to founder-led businesses — where the founder is also the original visionary, the relationship hub, and the operational center.

entries.name: Going to Market
entries.summary: The point at which a business actively starts engaging buyers — and the moment the seller's leverage peaks or collapses depending on preparation.

entries.name: Holdback
entries.summary: Money the buyer keeps from your sale price for a set period — in case something goes wrong after closing.

entries.name: Indemnification
entries.summary: Your contractual promise to make the buyer whole if something you said about the business turns out to be wrong.

entries.name: Independent Sponsor
entries.summary: A buyer who finds the deal first, then raises the capital to do it — flexible, often credible, but harder to validate than a traditional PE buyer.

entries.name: Key-Person Risk
entries.summary: The risk that the business depends too heavily on one person — usually the owner, sometimes a key employee — to keep functioning.

entries.name: LOI (Letter of Intent)
entries.summary: The non-binding document where a buyer puts their offer in writing — and where most of the deal actually gets decided.

entries.name: Management Buy-In (MBI)
entries.summary: When an outside executive (or team) buys the business and steps in to run it — common with search funders and turnaround buyers.

entries.name: Management Buyout (MBO)
entries.summary: When your existing management team buys the business from you — sometimes the cleanest exit, sometimes the most complicated.

entries.name: Material Adverse Change (MAC)
entries.summary: A clause that lets the buyer walk away from the deal if something really bad happens to your business between LOI and closing.

entries.name: NDA (Non-Disclosure Agreement)
entries.summary: The first document a buyer signs before seeing your numbers — and the only thing protecting your business from competitive damage.

entries.name: Owner Dependency
entries.summary: How much the business needs you personally to keep running — the single biggest valuation killer in small-business sales.

entries.name: Owner Dependency Discount
entries.summary: The price cut buyers apply when the business depends too much on you — usually 20%–40% off the multiple.

entries.name: PE Roll-Up
entries.summary: A private equity strategy that buys many small businesses in the same industry and combines them into one larger company — often the buyer behind small-business sales today.

entries.name: Pre-Market Action Plan
entries.summary: A ranked, sequenced list of the specific work to do before going to market — one of the four core deliverables of an Exit Desk report.

entries.name: Pre-Market Preparation
entries.summary: The work done before going to market to maximize valuation, attract better buyers, and reduce the risk of a deal falling apart in diligence.

entries.name: Quality of Earnings (QoE)
entries.summary: A formal report that rebuilds your earnings from scratch — what serious buyers commission before they close.

entries.name: Recasting Financials
entries.summary: Restating your financial statements to show what the business really earns — the work behind every credible add-back.

entries.name: Recurring Revenue
entries.summary: Revenue that automatically continues each period under contract or subscription — the highest-quality form of revenue for valuation purposes.

entries.name: Rep and Warranty Insurance
entries.summary: A policy that covers the buyer if they discover problems after closing — and lets you keep more of your sale price up front.

entries.name: Re-trade
entries.summary: When a buyer lowers the price after the LOI is signed — usually citing something they 'found' during diligence.

entries.name: Rollover Equity
entries.summary: When the seller keeps a piece of the business by reinvesting some of the sale proceeds into the new ownership structure.

entries.name: SDE (Seller's Discretionary Earnings)
entries.summary: The earnings number that matters for most small businesses. Includes the owner's salary, benefits, and personal expenses.

entries.name: SDE vs EBITDA
entries.summary: Which earnings number applies to your business — and why most small businesses use SDE, not EBITDA.

entries.name: Search Fund Buyer
entries.summary: An entrepreneur-investor pair raising capital specifically to buy and run one small business — a fast-growing buyer type for $1M–$10M deals.

entries.name: Sellability / Exit Readiness
entries.summary: The measure of how prepared your business is to be sold — and what buyers will see when they evaluate it.

entries.name: Seller Financing
entries.summary: When you, the seller, lend the buyer part of the purchase price — common in small-business deals, and not always optional.

entries.name: Strategic Buyer
entries.summary: A company in your industry buying yours for synergy, not just financial return — usually pays the highest price.

entries.name: Strategic vs Financial Buyer
entries.summary: The two big categories of business buyers — and why knowing which one you're talking to changes everything.

entries.name: Transferability
entries.summary: How easily your business — its customers, operations, contracts, and relationships — can move to a new owner.

entries.name: Value Drags
entries.summary: The structural features of your business that knock down what buyers will pay — the exact opposite of value drivers, and usually fixable.

entries.name: Value Drivers
entries.summary: The specific characteristics of your business that make buyers willing to pay a higher multiple — and what to invest in before going to market.

entries.name: Value Gap
entries.summary: The difference between what your business is worth today and what you need it to be worth to fund your retirement — and the work required to close it.

entries.name: Working Capital Peg
entries.summary: The amount of operating cash and inventory you have to leave in the business at closing — often a surprise to sellers.
